data.title: Samsara: When Compelling Growth Creates Concerning Valuation
data.description:
Editor's note: Seeking Alpha is proud to welcome Kamil Magomedov as a new contributing analyst. You can become one too! Share your best investment idea by submitting your article for review to our editors. Get published, earn money, and unlock exclusive SA Premium access. Click here to find out more »

Thesis:

Samsara Inc. (NYSE:IOT) is a rapidly growing IoT company focused on fleet management, asset tracking, and connected operations across logistics-heavy industries. Its strong revenue growth over recent fiscal years and recent profitability inflection have contributed to significant investor enthusiasm and a premium valuation. While Samsara’s strong strategic positioning and growth potential justify a certain premium, I believe the current market price already reflects optimistic assumptions regarding its expansion. BMO Capital raised its price target to $54, for example, which seems overly bullish and is embedded in aggressive expectations that may not fully account for potential risks Samsara is facing. Although the stock may see further short-term movement, I believe its valuation already reflects much of its long-term potential, leaving limited upside and warranting a sell recommendation.

Strong Momentum:

Samsara’s unique competitive advantage is the mix of hardware and software in its IoT applications. While many competitors, such as Fleet Complete and Geotab, provide exclusively GPS trackers or dashcams for fleet management Samsara also provides AI-powered software which greatly enhances its asset-tracking services. One notable example is its use of AI in the detection of dangerous driving habits, providing managers with insights into overall driver safety. While 98% of revenue is derived from subscriptions and only 2% from hardware sales, its integrated IoT services prove to be superior to those of its competitors - which is reflected heavily in its growth.

With revenue growths being 52.3%, 43.7% and 33.3% in FY2023, FY2024 and FY2025 respectively, optimistic forecasts are not entirely unjustified. These projections have been further supported by the company’s inflection into positive normalized net income and EBITDA in FY2024 - a milestone that reassured investors that Samsara’s historically negative margins were transitionary rather than structural. Notably, all of this was achieved with minimal leverage - no debt and only $82m worth of lease liabilities.

Samsara has outperformed consensus estimates for revenue, EBITDA and free cash flow growth over the last 5 quarters with its most recent 8K filing for Q1 FY2026 showing revenue of $366.9m, beating expectations by 4.31%, and normalized EBITDA of $56.21m, beating expectations by 78.21%. The revenue figure specifically represents a 31% YoY growth.

Moreover, in its Q4 FY2025 report, Samsara disclosed total Remaining Performance Obligations (RPOs) of $2,651.2m and current RPOs (cRPOs) of $1,253.8m. The total cRPO is nearly equivalent to its entire FY2025 revenue, providing a strong foundation for continued high growth. The company’s client diversification, with no single customer accounting for over 10% of revenue in any of the past three fiscal years, further reinforces the resilience of its business model and positions Samsara as a high-growth company with strong momentum.

Forecasted Growth:

Samsara’s growing number of $100K+ ARR customers and net retention rates of 115% and 120%, for core and large customers respectively, are some main drivers behind its high growth and demonstrate the increasing adoption of its services by large corporations. With 86.6% of its revenue coming from the United States, the company is well-positioned in a region where the fleet telematics industry is expected to experience a CAGR of 11.9 -14.3%. The industry growth is driven not only by companies’ desire to improve their fleet efficiency, but also by increasing regulations on fleet safety and environmental impacts. One of the biggest, but more long-term drivers of growth, is the increasing implementation of EV transportation into distribution networks. EVs require enhanced monitoring of battery status, charging cycles, and available chargers, necessitating both hardware and software solutions - precisely the area where Samsara excels. Recent partnerships with Rivian and Fraikin, with the former having a fleet size of 60,000 vehicles across Europe, further emphasized Samsara’s potential and catapulted growth forecasts even higher.

It is clear that Samsara benefits from a combination of strong intrinsic and extrinsic factors driving its growth, which is best exemplified on a comparable basis. Consensus data (taken from Capital IQ) shows astronomical forecasted growth both in terms of revenue and profitability. Revenue is expected to deliver a strong 20%+ annual growth performance over the next 3 calendar years, with normalised EBITDA showing an even stronger 30%+ annual growth.

The only caveat to this is Samsara’s own guidance for Q2 FY2026, which reported an estimated revenue of $373m. While this represents a 24% YoY growth, it marks a significant deceleration from the 31% YoY revenue growth of the first quarter. This caused short-term uncertainty about Samsara’s position, although the market maintains an overall optimistic perspective (as demonstrated by the graph above).

Before its recent press release, I was concerned about Samsara’s potential volatility as per the turmoil post-US elections. Samsara has a 3Y beta value of 1.64 (given by Capital IQ), meaning it is highly susceptible to market fluctuations, and also works closely with the fleeting industry which is facing a lot of uncertainty with the inconclusive US tariff policies. Given that a large percentage of its revenue comes from North America, Samsara positions itself as highly dependent on a stable and growing US economy, with economic downturns being disproportionally damaging to its growth. Although the company delivered a strong performance in Q1 FY2026, its more tempered Q2 guidance suggests that the effects of recent market turbulence may simply be lagging, rather than absent. Q2 results will likely offer a clearer indication of whether Samsara is meaningfully insulated from macroeconomic instability, though they may not be conclusive (other factors, such as seasonal fluctuation for the fleeting industry in the US, may also be the cause of the underwhelming projections).

Valuation:

Samsara does not perform well on a comparable basis, with Revenue, EBITDA and FCF multiples (all LTM) showing significant overvaluation. The EV/LTM CFO ratio of 154.0x further emphasizes its poor cash flows, which are mainly caused by net losses ($155m in FY2025). While it is expected for a high growth company to often underperform in these metrics, these numbers exemplify the gravity of expectations for Samsara’s future. It is clear that underwhelming growth would have a catastrophic impact on Samsara’s valuation, as it does not yet have a strong foundation that would justify its current enterprise value in the absence of rapid expansion.

One factor that may help mitigate the risk of falling short of investors’ expectations is Samsara’s conservative balance sheet ($82m worth of lease liabilities, with no other debt). While not a significant metric, its EV/Net Debt multiple is (5.2x) compared to the median of 1.1x of its peers. I believe it should be mentioned that its historic growth has been almost entirely organic, and future decisions regarding increasing leverage in order to further accelerate growth may be made. While Samsara has clearly favored a low-leverage approach to date, turning to debt financing as a means to accelerate growth remains a potential, although likely last resort, option for meeting future performance expectations.

On an intrinsic level, I believe Samsara may be at a disadvantage in having high structural R&D expenses. Due to its specialization in both software and hardware solutions (although only about 2% of the revenue comes from sales of the former) it needs to remain technologically competitive in both, while maintaining a high degree of integration between the two. Its LTM R&D margin of 23.2% was higher than both the mean and median of its peers, of 17.6% and 18.6% respectively. While it is difficult to definitively say whether or not this is the case, due to the different stages of development between the companies, this would be one of my more bearish assumptions.

In my DCF valuation, I was cautiously generous with Samsara’s forecasted growth, estimating an annual revenue growth rate of 30%+ until FY2028 and 20%+ until FY2032, which, I believe, is justified given the previously mentioned intrinsic and extrinsic factors. I forecasted a steep profitability margin expansion of ~5% per year throughout the late 2020s, which is in line with the historic growth of Samsara’s peers, and a terminal EBITDA margin of 25-26% which is slightly more optimistic than the median EBITDA margin of 24.3% (based on comparable companies). I maintained a profit margin of 79-80%, which is in line with the 79% margin Samsara achieved in Q1 FY2026. A realistic but optimistic terminal growth rate of 3.0% and a more conservative WACC of 10.4%, derived from comparable companies, give a final valuation of $45.52 per share. This suggests Samsara is fairly valued on an intrinsic basis, (with the closing price being $45.10 on June 6th, 2025).

While I remain confident with Samsara’s growth given its historical performance and strong foundation for continuous growth, I remain cautious about the potential volatility of its valuation. Samsara’s valuation is almost entirely dependent on meeting investor growth expectations, so failing to meet the consensus estimates would likely result in a disproportionally large investor pullback. This was best exemplified on the day of its press release for its financial results for Q1 FY2026 when the stock price fell over 10% in the trading after hours. Although it recovered the next day, this signifies Samsara’s sensitivity to falling short of investor expectations.

Risks:

Currently, ~87% of Samsara’s revenue comes from the United States. International growth has been relatively slow - in FY2023, only 10.8% of revenue came from outside the U.S., although there have been notable developments such as the previously mentioned partnerships with Rivian and Fraikin. While I accounted for international growth in my forecasts, it may expand at a faster rate than anticipated. Given that material traction in European markets has only begun to emerge over the past 12–24 months, forecasting the pace and scale of international expansion remains inherently uncertain. If Samsara successfully accelerates its global rollout, especially in high-potential regions like Europe, this could introduce upside risk to my valuation and result in the stock being undervalued relative to its true long-term potential.

Additionally, a core part of my thesis assumes that Samsara is vulnerable to macroeconomic headwinds, particularly due to its exposure to North American logistics and transportation. However, recent performance has not yet shown a clear impact of ongoing economic uncertainty, and the company may prove to be more insulated than expected. If that proves true, consistently meeting market expectations would become significantly more achievable, reducing the risk of a disproportionate investor pullback. The upcoming Q2 FY2026 results will likely serve as a decisive indicator of whether Samsara’s resilience to macroeconomic volatility is genuine or simply delayed and could introduce further upside risk to my valuation.

Conclusion:

While Samsara’s growth and strategic positioning are undeniably strong, I believe the current valuation already reflects much of the company's premium. Further bullish price targets appear to rely on aggressive assumptions that discount the risks tied to macroeconomic exposure and the company’s heavy reliance on consistently exceeding investor expectations. On a comparable basis, Samsara trades at significantly elevated revenue, EBITDA, and cash flow multiples relative to peers despite generating weak profits and cash flows. This disconnect highlights how much of its valuation is based on forward-looking optimism rather than present-day fundamentals.

Although I recognize the potential for continued strong growth, I view the market’s optimism as overly enthusiastic. Many of the scenarios required to justify higher valuations depend on sustained execution without missteps - a high bar for any company, let alone one still solidifying its profitability. For these reasons, I believe Samsara’s risk-reward profile at current levels is skewed, and its valuation leaves little margin for error. As such, I believe the best course of action is to sell or avoid initiating a position at current levels, as the stock’s valuation leaves limited long-term upside and considerable downside risk if execution falters.

data.title: FRP Holdings: Too Small To Invest In
data.description:
I rate FRP Holdings, Inc. (NASDAQ:FRPH) a Hold, for capital appreciation focused investors who are looking for long term buy and hold investments. FRP or “Florida Rock Properties” is an odd Florida based real estate company that develops, builds, manages, and owns industrial, multifamily, and commercial properties, using the cash flow from mining royalties. All of their properties are focused on the east coast of the United States.

It doesn’t pay a dividend, and its stock performance matches the real estate indexes over the long term. It occasionally outperforms the index. In my opinion, their lack of focus on one particular type of real estate is a weakness. One could argue they are diversified, but I would be concerned that they don’t have a particular expertise, therefore the competition could beat them in everything they do. Also, the properties are all located within the same geography on the eastern states, which does not promote diversification.

Therefore, in my experience, I don’t see a value in buying the stock.

The most important factors that influence my opinion of the stock are:

  • Does not pay a dividend.
  • Lags behind other alternatives such as real estate indexes, S&P500 & NASDAQ
  • Year to date, the stock is down -10.8%, one year -9.1%
  • Lack of focus in property type.
  • Thin stock volume, not covered by SA Quant, or Wall Street
  • Market cap of $522 million, which lacks scale and liquidity, creating a competitive disadvantage in real estate.

Seeking Alpha Quant does not cover the stock.

Real Estate Holding Company

Headquartered in Jacksonville, Florida, FRP Holdings, Inc. is a real estate business operating with four main segments: Industrial and Commercial, Mining Royalty Lands, Development, and Multifamily.

The Industrial and Commercial segment owns, leases, and manages commercial properties.

The Mining Royalty Lands segment leases and manages mining royalties owned by the company in Florida, Georgia, and Virginia. The company owns 14 quarries, 16,650 acres, in Florida, Georgia and Virginia. The quarries are subject to mining royalty leases with Vulcan Materials, Martin Marietta, Cemex, Argos, and The Concrete Company.

The Development segment owns and monitors the use of parcels of land that are in various stages of development; and acquires, constructs, and constructs new apartment, retail, warehouse, and office buildings.

The Multifamily segment owns, leases, and manages buildings through joint ventures.

The chart below shows how the 4 business segments break out. Key takeaways are mining is clearly the most valuable segment, accounting for approximately half of the company, followed by multifamily, which makes up less than 20%.

From an operational perspective, the company has performed well over time. The chart below shows net operating income (NOI) growing 17.2% CAGR for mining and royalty over the past 4 years. Industrial and commercial shows a CAGR of 33%, and Multifamily 31%. While these results are great, the stock isn’t matching the same growth, in my view.

The basic corporate strategy is investing the cash coming from mining to build rentable properties. FRP has generated a net operating income growth of 18.7% CAGR since 2018, which is remarkable in my view. Rather than issue a dividend, they continue to reinvest in building properties to increase rental income.

Q1 2025 Earnings Results

On May 12, the company declared Q1 earnings with the following financial highlights:

  • Total Revenues $10.3 million, up 1.7%
  • Net Income of $1.7 million up 31%
  • NOI of $9.4 million up 10%
  • Multifamily segment’s pro rata NOI up 3%
  • Industrial and Commercial segment NOI declined -2% due to an eviction and write-off of one tenant
  • Mining Royalty Lands segment's NOI up 19%

Overall, the first quarter posted extraordinary results. The only downside was the operating profit decreasing -19% due to the default of an industrial tenant. The company has other projects in the works that are at different stages in their journey. This will provide a future pipeline of rental income.

Performance To Date

An investment of $10,000 in FRP over 10 years would have grown to $18,570, which would have slightly underperformed the Real Estate Sector Index (XLRE) at $19,300 but beat the Vanguard Real Estate Index ETF (VNQ) at $16,850. This prompts the question, why bother? If the investment isn’t beating the benchmark, what would need to change in the future to make it worth holding? In my opinion, FRP would need to start paying a dividend to change this dynamic and attract a different type of investor.

Let’s assume the main reason an investor would buy FRPH is for capital appreciation, since there is no income component. Then, instead of comparing it to the Real Estate Sector, it would be fair to compare it to other choices such as the S&P500 (SPY) or NASDAQ (QQQ). In both cases, an investor would be better off vs. holding FRPH.

An investment of $10,000 in FRPH over 10 years severely underperforms the S&P500 and NASDAQ as seen in the chart below.

Valuation

I compared FRP to 3 other real estate operating companies, including Kennedy-Wilson Holdings, Inc. (KW), LandBridge Company LLC (LB) and Vonovia SE (OTCPK:VONOY).

FRPH was the smallest. All others had market caps over $1 billion. In fact, VONOY was almost $28 billion. Size has its advantages in real estate, such as getting loans, having experienced construction teams, and developing expertise.

None of the holding companies beat the real estate sector indexes. In fact, KW had declined -59% on a total return basis over 10 years.

Outlook

FRP Holdings will continue to grow as they reinvest money back into the company. Based on their current format, they will continue to build a mix of industrial, commercial, and multifamily properties over time building their portfolio. Over time, they may decide at some point to declare a dividend at which time, they would become a different investment. Until then, the company continues to build.

The current real estate market is in a good place in my view. It has lived through the interest rate hiking cycle; properties were devalued and had reached a bottom. Now, we’re in an expansionary phase with interest rates being cut, sort of. There may be another cut or two by the end of the year, according to Fed watchers. This would benefit FRPH in lower cost of borrowing.

Risks To Rating

The main risk to FRP now is construction delays in their new builds, and obstacles to collecting either rents or mining royalties. In Q1 the financials were impacted by one tenant who defaulted. Given the size of the company, the risk of tenants not paying is more severe as the income makes up a larger percentage of cash flow.

Bottom Line

If you currently do not own FRPH, I recommend a Hold at this time. There are no immediate catalysts to driving share price higher than other more attractive alternatives, such as the S&P or NASDAQ for capital appreciation.

FRPH does not pay a dividend, therefore it is not an income strategy. The company reinvests all the cash it makes back into the company, building more properties to rent out. In my experience, it is a mistake to build different types of buildings such as industrial and multifamily. I believe its better to specialize, since there are so many competitors out there that are specialists.

If you do own FRPH, consider what your main objective is. If it's capital appreciation, your capital will be better invested in the S&P500 such as SPY or in the NASDAQ such as QQQ.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: S&P Global: Why I Decided To Trim My Position
data.description:
S&P Global (NYSE:SPGI) is the type of company that attracts long-term minded investors. The company's Indices and Ratings segments are considered by many as two of the widest-moat businesses in the market, and even better, they come with attractive secular tailwinds.

That said, both segments should see meaningful deceleration in the near term, while the rest of the company is lower quality and seems quite inefficient.

What should investors do in light of this mixed setup? Let's dive in.

Introduction To S&P Global

S&P Global is primarily known for its leading position in credit ratings, alongside Moody's (MCO), and its popular proprietary indices, including the S&P 500.

Over the years, S&P Global used its internal knowhow and data to build (and acquire) ancillary businesses, which are currently operating under the Market Intelligence, Commodity Insights, and Mobility segments.

Most investors would agree that the core Indices and Ratings segments are elevating above the rest when it comes to moat, growth prospects, and overall quality, and that's reflected in those segment's 60%+ operating margins.

On the flip side, most investors would also agree that the rest of the business is operating in highly competitive industries, without significant barriers to entry or differentiation.

As a result, the two core segments, while only responsible for about 40% of revenues, are generating over 55% of the profits, and in reality it's even higher than that as S&P Global pays bonuses partially based on consolidated results, which means Market Intelligence employees are benefitting from Ratings results as well.

In two years of covering S&P Global, I maintained a bullish view, and so far, it seems to have been the wrong decision, as S&P Global is underperforming the market, and its closest peer over that stretch:

In my mind, 2025 was set up to be the year of outperformance for the company, until the recent macro developments, specifically regarding tariffs, which was cited as the key reason for the recent guidance cut.

Strong Results In Q1 Overshadowed By Guidance Cut

One of the key debates entering 2025 was the extremely tough growth comp in the core segments, which experienced elevated growth in 2024. I argued that worries about pull-forward are exaggerated, and viewed that as a key reason to buy the stock ahead of a better-than-expected year.

Well, I think we can say the procedure succeeded but the patient died.

In Q1, revenue grew 8%, with 15% growth in Indices, 9% growth in Mobility and Commodity Insights, 8% growth in Ratings, and 5% growth in Market Intelligence. All segments achieved growth levels that were significantly above the original guidance for the yar, and did so with margins that were in line or higher than guide.

As I expected, S&P Global's initial outlook for the year was on the conservative side. However, during the first four months of the year, and primarily since 'Liberation Day' in early April, the macro environment changed dramatically.

The enthusiastic business sentiment to start the year, which was predicated on the expectation for Trump's administration to be much more business-friendly than the previous one, quickly changed to worries about a trade war, uncertainty, and concerns about a rising deficit.

This led S&P Global to cut guidance on both growth and margins:

In addition to significantly reducing the growth expectations for two of its most important segments, in Indices and Ratings:

This pessimistic outlook for the rest of the year overshadowed the better-than-expected results in Q1, and justifiably gave investors pause.

Near-Term Outlook Is Concerning

Looking ahead, there are several areas I'm watching closely.

Issuance - Negative

Management already cut guidance, and it could be argued that they've placed a floor. However, they said they expect billed issuance to be flat for the year, expecting double-digit declines in Q2, and then a flat second half.

Meanwhile, April saw 19% declines, and comps aren't getting much easier until the end of the year. It's also worth noting that Moody's talked about a low-to-mid-single-digit decline in issuance, a more pessimistic range.

To me, if the ratings business experiences a significant revenue decline in Q2, the stock is likely to respond badly.

Indices - Positive

Since the time of guidance, April 22nd, the S&P 500 is up close to 15%. Based on the S&P 500's performance since the end of Q1, I expect average AUM in Q2 to be slightly below Q1, but not that much lower.

I estimate that if markets stay flat from here, the Indices segment will experience double-digit growth this year, significantly higher than the 6% guide.

Investor Day & Portfolio Strategy - Neutral

S&P Global is set to hold an Investor Day in November, where it is expected to provide a three-year plan, just like it did in 2022, with most of 2022's targets already achieved.

There are several interesting subjects on the table, including the Mobility divestiture, further portfolio optimization opportunities, and growth acceleration opportunities in Market Intelligence.

On the first topic, I believe that the near-term impact of the Mobility divestiture will be value-destructive. I don't think it will fetch a 30x P/E in a sale, and that's where S&P Global currently trades. It might be too simplistic, but that's an uncertainty to keep in mind.

On further portfolio optimization, I think that investors are becoming a bit frustrated with management. The IHS Markit acquisition is viewed by many as a poor move, and S&P Global seems to always be on the lookout for both tuck-in acquisitions (like the recently announced TeraHelix one), and possible divestitures. At this point, I think investors would love to see S&P Global just aggressively increasing exposure to its core wide-moat segments.

Lastly, Market Intelligence is a key focus point, given its size (32% of revenue, 22% of profit). Competitive pressures are rising, and while management sounds bullish on a looming acceleration driven in better sales motions and the Chief Client Officer role, investors remain skeptical. The investor day targets for this segment will tell a lot about its future and how it should be priced.

Valuation

The combination of those near-term catalysts, in my view, is net-net slightly leaning negative. As such, I would want valuation to reflect that.

At $506 a share, S&P Global trades at 30 times forward earnings, in line with its historical average, and at the typical discount below Moody's and MSCI (MSCI).

Consensus estimates see S&P Global growing revenues by 5% this year, and 7% next year, while EPS is expected to grow 8% in 2025, and 12% in 2026.

So, what we're getting at current price is a company priced at fair valuation, ahead of what should be a relatively bad quarter, specifically in the all-important Ratings business.

Meanwhile, consensus is already pricing acceleration in 2026, which is reasonable, but also doesn't leave much room for upside surprises.

Conclusion

S&P Global is an amazing company, and about 55% of its profit comes from wide-moat business lines that justify very high valuation multiples.

However, management's focus on other lines of business, alongside macro uncertainty, and relatively optimistic consensus estimates, leave little room for positive surprises.

Meanwhile, I expect to see disappointing results in the second quarter, and the stock trades as if everything is 'business-as-usual'.

I don't like this setup, which has led me to trim my position, and mandates a downgrade to 'Hold'.

data.title: Undercovered Dozen: Kraken Robotics, Honeywell, Blue Owl Capital And More
data.description:
The Undercovered Dozen is a weekly Seeking Alpha editor-curated series highlighting 12 articles on lesser covered stocks from the previous seven days. We hope this provides ideas and inspires discussion among the community.

Today, we're looking at articles published between June 6–June 12.

Take a look at what these less-covered stocks might hold for you. And please join the conversation below to share what you think: Are any of these worth following up on? And are there other undercovered ideas that you like?

Kraken Robotics: A Canadian Small-Cap That Shows Promise

Shri Upadhyaya | Strong Buy

If I told you about a robotics company that has surged over 6x in the last three years and revenues more than tripling during the same time, it would leave your head scratching, as the news clearly hasn't done justice (We all know about Tesla's Optimus or Boston Dynamics' Atlas that has kept investors focused but neither have provided an opportunity for retail investors to profit from).

That's the world of investing, isn't it?

What captivates the public rarely provides the opportunity for market-beating returns, and Kraken Robotics' (OTCQB:KRKNF) (TSXV:PNG:CA) story proves a point: many times, the biggest gains hide where the spotlight isn’t.

Being late to the party doesn't mean the story is over. As a small-cap company, it could have a long run ahead of itself. In fact, as I discovered, even with the stock skyrocketing in the last three years, the story is only getting started.

DIVO: The Covered Call ETF I Dislike The Least Still Beats SCHD

Gary Gambino | Buy

My last article got a lot of engagement on Seeking Alpha. I'm happy to say the discussion in the comments was mostly high quality. I expected a lot of basic "income vs. total return" debate, but it seems like most readers are smart enough to realize there are trade-offs between the two approaches based on different priorities. So, while I have not been convinced to abandon the total return approach myself, I did get some great feedback on other covered call ETFs I missed that investors might want to consider if they want to follow the call-writing strategy.

Foremost among these in age and assets under management is the Amplify CWP Enhanced Dividend Income ETF (NYSEARCA:DIVO). There are some key differences between this fund and the other covered call ETFs I reviewed in the last article that appear to have helped its performance. DIVO even stands out above a popular stock-only ETF that is a favorite of many income seeking investors on Seeking Alpha, the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (NYSEARCA:SCHD). The superior stock selection of DIVO, combined with some modest call writing, has resulted over time in better total return than SCHD with a higher yield.

Honeywell: Key Acquisitions And The Spin-Off Can Result In Upside

Sivanand Birusumanti | Buy

Honeywell International (NASDAQ:HON) is a global conglomerate that provides services to various industries to improve safety, sustainability, and efficiency. Established in 1906 by Mark Honeywell, the company has 102,000 employees operating across four segments: Aerospace, Building Automation, Industrial Automation, and Energy & Sustainability Solutions. The company will fully restructure its business into three independent businesses by late next year to improve focus on their respective strengths and enhance value to shareholders.

Honeywell's spinoff will be a pivotal growth driver for the company. With the Automation and Aerospace segments set to split, the independent Aerospace business can easily maximize its resources to take advantage of the growing global aerospace parts manufacturing market in the next 10 years. With Aerospace being Honeywell's largest revenue segment, market expansion could translate to long-term margin growth for Honeywell. The company is also poised for sustainable organic growth with smart acquisitions to be completed in the coming year. Despite being vulnerable to economic downturns, Honeywell has solid growth potential, strong fundamentals, and is still trading at a discount. This is an opportune phase for investors to buy Honeywell shares from a long-term perspective.

Kimbell Royalty Partners: A Double-Digit Yield And Exceptional Business Model For Long-Term Investors

Symeon Mavroudis | Buy

The investment choice of Kimbell Royalty Partners (KRP) is most suitable for investors who plan to hold their shares for the long term.

The company operates a successful business model while maintaining strong financial fundamentals and a dividend policy that benefits shareholders.

The stock faces limited potential for upward movement because its market capitalization stands at $1.6B and royalties sector growth remains moderate.

The company maintains solid financials which indicate its high dividend (14% yield, $1.88 FWD) will remain secure even if it experiences a minor reduction in the future.

The May 7 acquisition of 50% Series A Preferred Shares initially reduced distributable cash flow, but it will boost future redemption value and decrease medium- to long-term capital costs while demonstrating management's dedication to shareholder value.

The combination of operational excellence with management commitment to shareholder value creates investor and shareholder confidence which drives solid long-term investment choices.

Blue Owl Capital: Strong Brand Name, Bright Future

David Johnson | Buy

I rate Blue Owl Capital Inc. (NYSE:OWL) a Buy, for investors who are looking for long-term buy and hold investments in the financial services sector. Blue Owl went public in 2021, attracting a great deal of attention with their high yielding alternative investments. Q1 results showed growth in AUM and related revenues and fees; however, some one-time acquisition expenses knocked down the earnings per share, causing a miss vs. estimates. This earnings miss which has occurred for the past two quarters has caused the stock to pause, however I expect future quarters to show much higher earnings, unencumbered by the unusual costs.

VICI Properties: Always Dull At VICI-Land, And That's Good

YR Research | Buy

It's been over three years since VICI Properties (NYSE:VICI) completed two of the most significant deals in its history, acquiring MGM Growth Properties and The Venetian.

In that same 2022 year, VICI received investment grade ratings from two major ratings agencies, and entered the S&P 500.

At that point in time, I bet most investors wouldn't believe if I told them the following three years will be almost uneventful.

And yet, I argue that this is a good thing.

Circle Internet: Scaling Beyond USDC Toward A Tokenized Economy Powerhouse

Mandela Amoussou | Hold

The Circle Internet Group (CRCL) IPO is finally here. The IPO price was $31, but CRCL quickly went up over 100% on trading debut and closed day one trading at $83.23 (168% higher than IPO price).

It’s been a long walk for Circle to this point, following the abandoned SPAC deal in 2022, and the long campaign that followed to position USDC (USDC-USD) as a fully transparent, compliant stablecoin within the emerging global financial infrastructure. Circle’s premier stablecoin USDC, currently the second most widely adopted stablecoin by market capitalization and transaction volume, boasts a ~$61.5 billion market cap and ~61.5 billion USDC in circulation and a ~29% of the total stablecoin market share.

Circle also issues a euro-backed stablecoin, EURC, which has also been gaining traction, especially recently as EURC surged to a record supply in circulation in April in the wake of intensifying tariff tensions between the U.S. and Europe and a weakening dollar likely fueling demand for euro-pegged digital assets. EURC has also benefited from Tether's withdrawal of its euro-backed stablecoin the Euro Tether amid the rollout of MiCA regulations across the European Union, which has seen a number of exchanges delisting USDT for E.U. users to comply with MiCA. EURC now holds the status as the go-to euro stablecoin option for regulated on-chain finance in Europe.

With the global institutionalization of stablecoins accelerating, Circle’s strong record of regulatory compliance means it could benefit significantly if a similar regulatory framework to EU’s MiCA takes shape in the U.S., particularly as the current administration moves closer to enacting the GENIUS Act. This would likely position Circle as one of the first issuers eligible to offer “Covered Stablecoins” under a formal regulatory regime.

NewLake Capital Partners: 'High' 12% Yield Backed By Net Cash And Net Leases

Julian Lin | Strong Buy

NewLake Capital Partners, Inc. (OTCQX:NLCP) has struggled amidst regulatory delays and increasing tenant strain. The market appears pessimistic regarding the company’s ability to sustain its dividend payout, leading to an elevated double-digit yield. Investors appear to be underappreciating the balance sheet and margin of safety here. The stock looks like it can survive even apocalyptic scenarios. NLCP remains my top income pick today.

SPMO: Trust The Process

MacroGirl | Buy

I have been closely following the Invesco S&P 500 Momentum ETF (SPMO) for some time now, initially highlighting its outstanding performance in February 2024 and subsequently recommending a buy in December. However, after the major March rebalancing, I downgraded my rating to "hold" and questioned its inclusion of Tesla (TSLA) at the expense of Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.A) (BRK.B).

With SPMO now comfortably at new all-time highs and performing just as well as ever, in this article I will re-assess my earlier doubts and why it may just be best to trust the fund's process (or methodology).

Grupo Aeroportuario del Centro Norte: A Strategic Player In The Mexican Sky

Eliana Scialabba | Buy

Grupo Aeroportuario del Centro Norte (NASDAQ:OMAB) is a company that no longer needs to prove anything: it manages 13 airports in Mexico –including the one in Monterrey, one of the most important outside the capital, and is strategically located in the north of the country, where today nearshoring is growing, and heavy industry is moving.

In 1Q25, it transported 6.43 million passengers (+9.1% YoY), and its commercial revenue per passenger increased 12.5% ​​to Ps. 66.3 million. Non-aeronautical revenue grew 20.9%, thanks to strong demand for VIP lounges (+80%) and stores (+51%). Its adjusted EBITDA was Ps. 2,372 B, with a margin of 74.9%, and operating cash flow reached Ps. 1,918 B (+25%).

Furthermore, it is distributing dividends of Ps. 4,500 B this year, implying a 4.6% yield, and maintains extremely low leverage (net debt/EBITDA of 0.96x). It may seem expensive at some multiples, but profitability and efficiency explain this premium.

In short, OMAB is a mature company with irreplaceable assets, top-tier margins, and a very clear operating logic: not growth for growth's sake, but growth well, with a focus on returns. For investors seeking quality, consistent income, and real assets, this is a very good defensive opportunity in a strategic sector that continues to soar.

Regenxbio's RGX-121 Could Become The New Standard Of Care In Hunter Syndrome

ELAM1 Consulting | Buy

Regenxbio (NASDAQ:RGNX) is a clinical-stage biopharmaceutical company focusing on gene therapies. Founded in 2008, the core technology of the company is the delivery of rescue genes using AAV payload. A growing number of approved drugs are based on this well established AAV approach. In May 2025, Regenxbio announced that the FDA will settle on the approval (PDUFA) of RGX-121 in the treatment of Hunter syndrom (HS) on November 9th 2025. In this article, we propose to review the Regenxbio submission and to explain why, at ELAM1, we think RGNX could be a Buy in advance of the FDA decision in November.

Red Cat Holdings: From Air To Sea Revenue Expansion In Autonomous Drones

Value Kicker | Strong Buy

I’ve written about Red Cat Holdings (NASDAQ:RCAT) in the past, discussing the long-term potential of its drone technology. This article serves as an update on the previous article, taking into account quarter results as well as the company’s exciting new product line. While the results of this quarter aren’t particularly exciting, Red Cat could be at an inflection point given all the potential catalysts ahead.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: SailPoint: Strength Is Now Priced In (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
With the stock markets cheering the initial frameworks of a U.S.-China trade deal and seemingly complacent on the possibility of a U.S. recession as well as bond market risks, I remain increasingly sensitive to valuations, especially among large-cap momentum stocks. I've taken advantage of the rally over the past few weeks to sell off a number of winners and rotate more of my portfolio into value stocks, cash, and short-term bonds.

SailPoint (NASDAQ:SAIL) is one of the holdings I've shed. The cybersecurity software company, which focuses on identity governance tools that help determine which users get access to what data, has been quite successful in its second act on the public markets. A recent beat-and-raise in Q1 helped SailPoint shares jump ~30%. The question for investors now is: is this the beginning of a broader rally for SailPoint, or is it time to lock in gains?

I last wrote a buy opinion on SailPoint in April, when the stock was trading closer to $17 per share. At the time, I had touted the company's many strengths (growth at scale, great growth/profitability balance, and unique product with fewer competitors relative to other SaaS plays) - but even in the high teens, I had cautioned that SailPoint's valuation was looking rich. Today, after the stock's sharp post-earnings rally, the valuation risk has magnified. I've been pleasantly surprised by the speed and magnitude of my gains on my SailPoint position, and am quite satisfied to lock in gains here. I'm downgrading SailPoint to a neutral rating.

To me, at current share prices SailPoint is a mixed bag of positives and negatives. On the bright side for this company:

  • SailPoint exhibits both growth at scale and great profitability balance. The company is growing revenue at a >20% clip despite hitting over $1 billion in annual run-rate revenue, a feat that very few tech companies can claim (in fact, SailPoint achieved accelerating growth in its most recent quarter, which is yet another tough feat in a tight macroeconomy). At the same time, it is managing ~10% pro forma operating margins, which puts SailPoint close to a "Rule of 40" threshold.
  • Unique product and universally applicable. Identity management is a service that every company needs in order to maintain strict data privacy controls on any network. Though SailPoint certainly has competitors, relatively few companies focus exclusively on identity management, meaning SailPoint has far fewer competitors than, say, CRM or HCM software companies.

At the same time, however, there are a number of risks to keep in mind:

  • Seat-based pricing may be at risk in a recessionary economy. Companies are slashing headcount (or at least not growing heads at the same pace as they used to in the past). With SailPoint pricing its products per user, this may mean that its net retention rates could see pressure.
  • Limited margin progress. Yes, SailPoint has great growth/profitability balance - but its operating margins haven't been expanding despite strong growth and what should flow through as economies of scale, indicating that the company's bottom-line expansion may be limited going forward.

To me, the biggest risk that SailPoint faces is its valuation. At current share prices near $23, SailPoint trades at a $12.97 billion market cap. After we net off the $228.1 million of cash on SailPoint's most recent balance sheet, the company's resulting enterprise value is $12.74 billion.

Meanwhile, for FY26 (the year for SailPoint ending in January 2026), SailPoint has upped its full-year revenue guidance to $1.034-$1.044 billion in revenue, or 20-21% y/y growth - which is 1 point of growth stronger on both ends of the range versus a prior $1.025-$1.035 outlook. Importantly, the company has also boosted its ARR growth expectations by two points to a 25-26% y/y range, while also indicating that its midpoint adjusted operating margin would also be slightly stronger at ~15.7%.

SailPoint now trades at 12.2x EV/FY25 revenue. The stock now trades on the noticeable high end of software/SaaS stocks that also are growing at a high teens/low 20s pace. On the optimistic side, SailPoint is still cheaper than perennially-expensive ServiceNow at ~15x forward revenue - but the stock is now more expensive than Datadog (DDOG) and Atlassian (TEAM), both also momentum stocks that have long been Wall Street favorites.

To me, this is a good juncture to sell off SailPoint, especially as execution risk in a tough macroeconomy remains high. Return to the sidelines here and wait for a better entry point in this stock before buying back in.

Q1 download

That being said, we still acknowledge that SailPoint has delivered terrific fundamental results - it's just that I believe most of this strength is already priced into the stock's recent rally, leaving little room for further upside. Let's now go through SailPoint's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q1 earnings summary is shown below:

SailPoint's revenue grew 23% y/y to $230.5 million, beating Wall Street's expectations of $225.2 million (+20% y/y) by a three-point margin. Perhaps even more impressively, SailPoint also accelerated five points versus just 18% y/y growth in Q4.

And as shown in the chart below, SailPoint's net revenue retention rates improved slightly to 115% - indicating that the average SailPoint customer is spending 15% more on the platform than they did in the prior year.

This is an impressive achievement in a slowing macroeconomy, where many seat-based software companies have reported pressure on dollar-based net retention rates as their clients' headcount growth slows and IT budgets are more carefully scrutinized. That being said, however, SailPoint does face the same macro risk as its peers, so we shouldn't treat past results as an indicator that SailPoint can continue executing to this level.

We note that one tailwind that SailPoint has on its side is the rise of AI agents. As more software companies release agentic AI features that their customers are letting loose to replace tasks traditionally performed by human employees, companies have needed additional security governance measures to control what data those agents have access to. These use-cases appear to be bolstering SailPoint's growth rates, as evidenced by its sequential improvements in revenue growth and net retention rates. SailPoint's guidance also suggests that revenue growth will continue accelerating throughout FY26.

We are disappointed, however, in the fact that SailPoint's pro forma operating margins remained flat y/y at 10.2%:

The company's guidance indicates that pro forma operating margins should improve to as high as 16.1% for the full year FY26, but at the moment it appears that SailPoint is spending aggressively to take advantage of its growth momentum, boosting its sales and marketing spending by 43% y/y to $164.5 million.

Key takeaways

To me, while I applaud SailPoint's terrific acceleration in growth (which the company is expecting to continue throughout FY26), I'm not comfortable holding onto this stock at a >12x forward revenue multiple, especially in a decaying macroeconomy which may interrupt SailPoint's recent growth trends. Expectations for the company are now quite high, and with SailPoint priced to perfection, I'm content here to lock in gains on my position and invest elsewhere until I can re-enter the stock at a more reasonable price.
